text:
What are your metrics for success?

After decades of nonstop hustling, I recently gave myself permission to pause and recalibrate. For the first time in a long while, I asked myself: what does success mean to me now?

In business, we obsess over metrics. But when it comes to our personal lives, we often forget to stop and get clear on what truly matters. In life as in business, if you don’t know what success looks like, it will be much harder to tell if you’re moving in the right direction.

The real risk isn’t falling short — it’s succeeding at the wrong thing.

What does success look like for you? What’s one metric you’ve stopped measuring, and one you’ve started?

Tariff headlines. Market swings. Policy uncertainty.

In volatile times, corporate strategy can—and often must—shift.

But a leader’s values shouldn’t.

We’re seeing it now: companies reevaluating supply chains, pausing expansion plans, or reconsidering how they allocate capital. These are rational responses to a shifting global environment.

How leaders make these decisions—and communicate them—matters just as much.

The best leaders lean into their core.

They act with clarity, consistency, and courage—because they know what they stand for.

That’s how trust is built.

And in a noisy, uncertain world, trust is everything.

I used to think the biggest risk in life was failing. But I learned the real risk is never trying.

Most people wait for the “perfect time.” Perfect skills. Perfect plan. Perfect confidence.

Truth? None of that exists. You build it on the way, not before you start.

Every person you admire today once felt unready. They just didn’t let that stop them.

Start the YouTube channel. Apply for that job. Ask for that promotion. Launch that side hustle. Post that idea.

You’ll be bad before you’re good. You’ll be scared before you’re confident. But one day, you’ll look back and realize — this messy, uncertain beginning… was the best part.

If this hit you even a little — it’s your sign to stop waiting and start building.

Because the world doesn’t need a perfect you. It needs a real you — in progress, trying anyway.

Have you noticed that beautiful people give compliments, rich people support you, athletes train you like themselves, and those who have nothing to be proud of look for ways to put you down?

Remember this — life will become easier :)

On a recent flight, a young man sitting next to me kept opening and closing his window shade. Every few minutes, up it went—sunlight streaming in. A few minutes later, down it went—darkness again. Over and over. It was like someone was flicking a light switch, thwarting my hopes for a quick nap.

I felt irritated at his thoughtlessness and was about to ask him to stop.

Then I paused. I reframed.

Instead of assuming he was inconsiderate, I asked myself—what else could be true? What if he was nervous about flying? What if this was his way of soothing himself?

That shift in perspective changed everything. I didn’t need to confirm my story; just considering a different possibility made me less annoyed. I felt more curious, more compassionate. I relaxed. His window shade routine no longer bothered me.

Reframing is a powerful tool. If we take a moment to pause and ask, "what else could be true?" we shift from frustration to curiosity—and that small shift can change everything.

As leaders, asking the right questions is critical.

Yes-or-no questions can end conversations quickly and cause disconnection. "Why" questions often make people feel defensive.

Instead, start questions with "what." This indicates openness and curiosity, leading to exploratory conversations. You may be surprised where the conversation leads.

If you're ever at a loss for words, ask: "What's next?"

Speaking up in meetings is often seen as a sign of confidence and leadership. However, sometimes it's the person who stays quiet and observes who has the most impact on the room.

I've been in countless pitch meetings over the last few years, and I've noticed that the people who don't speak up right away often have the most valuable insights to share.

If you're someone who tends to stay quiet in meetings, don't underestimate the power of observation. You may be able to spot opportunities or challenges that others have overlooked.

And when you do speak up, don't be afraid to ask basic questions. Sometimes, those draw out the best insights.
Just saw a LinkedIn Influencer with 'Organic Growth' written in the profile with 65K+ followers claiming that he can help you in growing your platform, copying the posts from other influencers.

Jobseekers, this one’s for you.
Every application, every interview, every follow-up… the pressure is immense.
And I know what you're thinking: Am I not good enough?
But let me tell you, this isn’t about you or your skills. It’s about a broken system where 60% of applicants never hear back.
Your mental health is not worth sacrificing for a system that doesn’t acknowledge your worth.
Please remember, taking care of yourself is the real priority.
Your dream job will come, but for now, breathe. 🌻
Looking for jobs on LinkedIn is like online dating: Full of promises, but in the end, you’re just left ghosted.
LinkedIn scams be like: 'Congratulations, you've been selected for a role you didn’t even apply for!' The catch? Pay Rs. 50,000 for the honor.
Next time when I'll be reading some LinkedIn Influencer's story, I am starting from the last line. If there's a link attached to it, it's most probably a fake one. Saves me time!

Every time I poured my heart into 5-6 rounds of interviews and faced rejection, it felt like a punch in the gut. The sleepless nights, the endless preparation, all for nothing.

But looking back, I realize it wasn’t nothing. It was the Universe’s way of saying, “Not this one, something better is on the way.”

Doors I thought I wanted to walk through were shut, only to have the right ones swing open.

If you feel the weight of rejection: trust that the closed doors are protecting you from something you can’t see right now.

Your path is being cleared for something even more beautiful.

To everyone who's still looking for a job... I see you. I feel you. 💔 Every rejection email feels like a punch in the gut, and every 'We'll get back to you' sounds more like 'You'll never hear from us.'
But you’re not alone. 🌸 Take breaks, breathe, and remember, your worth is not tied to an offer letter. 💥

Sometimes, we forget that a company’s brand name doesn’t define someone’s talent. Let’s stop attaching someone’s value to the company they work for. Freshers especially need to hear this—skills are what matter, not the size of the company behind them.

So when I left a toxic work environment, I told my manager a simple thing and felt so good 😯 'Hope your son gets a manager like you.' If you felt bad, that means you were a bad manager and now you know it. 🦀

We always ask - “Why are talented people leaving India?” Maybe this is why... Corruption in India doesn’t just steal money. It steals dignity. It steals humanity. It steals trust in the country we call home.

Layoffs everywhere. These are not just numbers. These are dreams, visas, rent payments, and parents who still think their child is doing “IT wali naukri.”
Your job isn’t your identity. Your company isn’t your safety net.
Keep believing in yourself — the economy may glitch, but your worth doesn’t.

We live in a world where… 'You need proof for stress.' 'You need a reason to not be a machine.'
And then there’s this email 👇 'Sir, I recently had a breakup… I need a short break.'
No fancy excuse. No fake illness. Just honesty.
Let’s build workplaces where vulnerability isn’t weakness — it’s respected as strength.

When “business continuity” becomes more important than human empathy. Someone lost a loved one, and the company said, 'We empathize, but can’t postpone your start date.'
If a company cannot accommodate grief, it’s not a workplace — it’s a machine.

“When you’re doing a 9-to-5 job, you can take tea breaks. Actors can’t.” – Kajol
Different battlefield, same fatigue. Every profession has its own struggle. Instead of comparing who has it worse, let’s normalize respect for all kinds of work.
When you go to bed tonight, think of where you stand today. Once upon a time, this was the life you prayed for. Don’t just chase what’s next — honor how far you’ve come. Whisper a small 'thank you.'

Being a Product Manager is not just about data, dashboards, or documents. It’s about confidence — to say no, to lead, to defend users, to admit mistakes. The roadmap matters, but your voice is your real product.
